strands.title: Number and Place Value
strands.description: Whole numbers to 1 000 000 and decimal place value to thousandths, including powers of ten and multiplicative place-value reasoning.

strands.title: Operations and Algebraic Thinking
strands.description: Multi-digit multiplication and division, order of operations, numerical expressions, and the meaning of the equals sign.

strands.title: Fractions
strands.description: Adding and subtracting fractions with unlike denominators, fraction-as-division, and multiplying fractions by whole numbers and fractions.

strands.title: Decimal Operations
strands.description: Comparing, rounding, adding, subtracting, multiplying, and dividing decimals to hundredths using place-value reasoning.

strands.title: Geometry and Measurement
strands.description: Volume of rectangular prisms, coordinate plane basics, and classification of two-dimensional figures in a hierarchy.

outcomes.title: Extend place value to millions and thousandths
outcomes.subject: mathematics
outcomes.description: The base-ten system is multiplicative in both directions: a digit in one place represents ten times what it represents one place to the right and one tenth of what it represents one place to the left. Students read, write, compare, and round whole numbers to 1 000 000 and decimals to thousandths. Multiplying a number by 10, 100, or 1000 shifts its digits left; dividing shifts them right. Powers of ten provide compact notation for these repeated factors.

outcomes.title: Multiply multi-digit whole numbers fluently
outcomes.subject: mathematics
outcomes.description: Students multiply multi-digit whole numbers using the standard algorithm, understanding each partial product as a place-value piece of the area model. A two-digit by two-digit product decomposes into four partial products: tens times tens, tens times ones, ones times tens, and ones times ones. Estimating first by rounding both factors gives a magnitude check that catches misplaced partial products, the most common multiplication error at this level.

outcomes.title: Divide multi-digit numbers by two-digit divisors
outcomes.subject: mathematics
outcomes.description: Division by a two-digit divisor builds on place-value sectioning: students find how many groups of the divisor fit in each place-value chunk of the dividend, recording partial quotients. The relationship dividend equals divisor times quotient plus remainder allows every division answer to be verified by multiplication. Quotients are interpreted in context, deciding whether to round up, drop, or report the remainder as a fraction.

outcomes.title: Evaluate expressions and reason about equality
outcomes.subject: mathematics
outcomes.description: The equals sign states that two expressions name the same value; it is not an instruction to compute an answer. Students evaluate numerical expressions using the order of operations with parentheses and brackets, write simple expressions from verbal descriptions, and solve for unknowns in equations like 8 + n = 14 by reasoning about balance rather than by reading left to right. An equation such as 7 = 3 + 4 is just as valid as 3 + 4 = 7.

outcomes.title: Add and subtract fractions with unlike denominators
outcomes.subject: mathematics
outcomes.description: Fractions can only be added or subtracted when they are expressed in the same-sized pieces. Students rewrite fractions with a common denominator — using equivalence, not a memorized rule — and then add or subtract numerators. Benchmark fractions support estimation: 7/12 plus 3/8 must be a little less than 1 because each addend is near 1/2. Students add and subtract mixed numbers and check the reasonableness of answers with number-line models.

outcomes.title: Multiply fractions and interpret fraction as division
outcomes.subject: mathematics
outcomes.description: A fraction is also a division: 3/4 means 3 divided by 4, the size of one share when 3 wholes are split among 4. Multiplying a fraction by a whole number scales copies of a unit fraction; multiplying a fraction by a fraction takes a part of a part, which the area model shows as overlapping shading. Multiplying by a fraction less than one produces a product smaller than the starting number — scaling, not shrinking the operation's validity — which breaks the whole-number intuition that multiplication always makes bigger.

outcomes.title: Compute with decimals to hundredths
outcomes.subject: mathematics
outcomes.description: Decimal addition and subtraction align place values, which means aligning the decimal points; appending zeros to equalize lengths changes nothing because 0.5 and 0.50 are equivalent. Decimal multiplication and division extend whole-number reasoning with powers of ten: 0.3 x 0.4 is 3 tenths of 4 tenths, twelve hundredths, 0.12. Students estimate first so the placement of the decimal point in a product or quotient is justified by magnitude reasoning rather than a digit-counting rule applied blindly.

outcomes.title: Understand and calculate volume of rectangular prisms
outcomes.subject: mathematics
outcomes.description: Volume measures the space a solid figure occupies in cubic units — how much fits inside a box. A rectangular prism (a box shape) packed with unit cubes has volume equal to the number of cubes in one layer times the number of layers, which gives the formulas V = l x w x h and V = B x h where B is the base area. Volumes of composite figures made of two non-overlapping prisms add. Students distinguish volume from surface area and choose appropriate cubic units for context.

outcomes.title: Graph points in the first quadrant
outcomes.subject: mathematics
outcomes.description: The coordinate plane locates points by an ordered pair: the first number gives horizontal distance from the origin along the x-axis and the second gives vertical distance along the y-axis. Order matters — (3, 5) and (5, 3) are different points. Students plot points, read coordinates from graphs, and interpret real-world relationships such as distance over time by reading coordinate values in context.

knowledge_components.name: Place value to 1 000 000 and powers of ten
knowledge_components.subject: mathematics

common_misconceptions.label: Multiplying by ten just adds a zero
common_misconceptions.description: Applies the 'add a zero' rule symbolically, which fails for decimals: claims 0.5 x 10 = 0.50.
common_misconceptions.remediation_hint: Reframe as digit shifting on a place-value chart: every digit moves one place left, the decimal point stays.

common_misconceptions.label: Longer decimal is larger
common_misconceptions.description: Concludes 0.125 is greater than 0.5 because it has more digits, treating the decimal part as a whole number.
common_misconceptions.remediation_hint: Compare place by place on a decimal place-value chart, padding with zeros: 0.500 vs 0.125.

common_misconceptions.label: Shorter decimal is larger
common_misconceptions.description: Overcorrects the longer-is-larger error, concluding 0.3 is always greater than 0.45 because tenths are bigger than hundredths.
common_misconceptions.remediation_hint: Place both numbers on the same hundredths grid so the comparison is visual before it is symbolic.

common_misconceptions.label: Second partial product not shifted
common_misconceptions.description: In the standard algorithm, writes the tens-row partial product without the place shift, e.g. 23 x 14 computed as 92 + 23 instead of 92 + 230.
common_misconceptions.remediation_hint: Connect each algorithm row to its area-model region so the tens row is visibly ten times larger.

common_misconceptions.label: Division always makes smaller
common_misconceptions.description: Assumes every quotient is smaller than the dividend, which later breaks for divisors less than one and blocks estimation checks.
common_misconceptions.remediation_hint: Pose sharing problems with divisor 1 and divisors between 0 and 1 informally: how many half-metre ribbons in 3 metres?

common_misconceptions.label: Zero skipped inside quotient
common_misconceptions.description: Omits interior zeros in the quotient, e.g. 612 / 6 = 12 instead of 102, because a place with no groups is skipped rather than recorded.
common_misconceptions.remediation_hint: Use partial quotients with an explicit place-value table so every place receives a digit, even zero.

knowledge_components.name: Order of operations with grouping symbols
knowledge_components.subject: mathematics

common_misconceptions.label: Strict left-to-right evaluation
common_misconceptions.description: Evaluates 3 + 4 x 2 as 14 by working strictly left to right, ignoring operation priority.
common_misconceptions.remediation_hint: Read the expression as a story — 3 plus 4 groups of 2 — so the grouping precedes the rule.

common_misconceptions.label: Equals sign means 'write the answer'
common_misconceptions.description: Treats = as a do-something signal: answers 12 for the blank in 8 + 4 = __ + 5 by computing the left side.
common_misconceptions.remediation_hint: Use a pan-balance model and true/false equations like 7 = 7 and 6 + 2 = 5 + 3 before solving for blanks.

knowledge_components.name: Rewrite fractions with a common denominator
knowledge_components.subject: mathematics

common_misconceptions.label: Only one part of the fraction scaled
common_misconceptions.description: Multiplies the denominator to match the common denominator but forgets to scale the numerator, e.g. rewrites 2/3 as 2/12.
common_misconceptions.remediation_hint: Show the strip being cut: quartering every third cuts the shaded pieces too — both numbers change together.

common_misconceptions.label: Adds numerators and denominators across
common_misconceptions.description: Computes 1/2 + 1/3 as 2/5, adding tops and bottoms separately without common pieces.
common_misconceptions.remediation_hint: Estimate first: both addends are at least 1/3, so the sum cannot be 2/5 which is less than 1/2.

common_misconceptions.label: Multiplication always makes bigger
common_misconceptions.description: Rejects or distrusts products smaller than a factor, e.g. doubts that 1/2 x 6 = 3, because whole-number multiplication always grew.
common_misconceptions.remediation_hint: Reframe multiplication as 'of': 1/2 of 6 is obviously 3 — connect the symbol to the scaling story.

common_misconceptions.label: Decimals right-aligned like whole numbers
common_misconceptions.description: Lines up the rightmost digits instead of the decimal points, computing 3.2 + 0.45 as 0.77.
common_misconceptions.remediation_hint: Estimate first (3.2 + about a half is about 3.7), then align on the place-value chart.

common_misconceptions.label: Decimal point placed by counting without magnitude check
common_misconceptions.description: Applies the count-the-decimal-places rule mechanically and accepts absurd magnitudes, e.g. 0.3 x 0.4 = 1.2.
common_misconceptions.remediation_hint: Anchor with estimation: 0.3 x 0.4 is less than half of 0.4, so the answer must be less than 0.2.

common_misconceptions.label: Adds dimensions instead of multiplying
common_misconceptions.description: Computes volume as l + w + h, or confuses volume with surface area when a net is shown.
common_misconceptions.remediation_hint: Build the prism from unit cubes layer by layer: one layer holds l x w cubes, and there are h layers.

knowledge_components.name: Plot and read points in the first quadrant
knowledge_components.subject: mathematics

common_misconceptions.label: Coordinates reversed
common_misconceptions.description: Plots (3, 5) by going up 3 and across 5, treating the ordered pair as (vertical, horizontal).
common_misconceptions.remediation_hint: Anchor the convention physically: walk along the hallway (x) before taking the stairs (y).
